Help & FAQ
Tap a question to expand. Still stuck? Use Send Feedback from the More menu and we will get back to you fast.
How do I add a new entry?
Tap the gold + button on your Dashboard or open the Journal tab. Pick the entry type that fits today: game, practice, hitting, fielding, weightlifting, reflection, or off day.
How does Coach Feedback work?
If your team is set up in My Team, your coach can send you direct messages that land as a card on your Dashboard. You also get a text on your phone with a link to read the full reply.
Can I switch between baseball and softball?
Yes. Open Settings and find the Sport toggle. The app re-themes live. Your entries stay, and the icons and stat categories adapt to the sport you pick.
How do I share progress with family?
Open the Share With Family card on your Dashboard. You can text, email, or copy a recap link. Your parents also get a weekly summary every Monday morning automatically.
How do I export my data?
Open Settings, then Backup & Restore, then Export. You get a file with everything: entries, goals, grades, photos. Save it somewhere safe like your phone, email, or a cloud drive.
Where do I manage my subscription?
For most players, billing is on your parent. If you need to change a card or cancel, ask them to open the email link Stripe sent. If you signed up as an adult on your own plan, you will see a Manage Billing button right inside Settings.
What if I forget my account or lose access?
Tap Send Feedback in the More menu and email support@mygrindapp.com with your name and your parent's email. We will recover it for you.
How do I contact support?
Tap Send Feedback in the More menu. It opens a pre-filled email to support@mygrindapp.com. We answer fast.

MYJOURNAL
Today's Grind
Defaults to today. Pick a past date if you forgot to log a missed day.
😴 OFF DAY ALREADY LOGGED · THIS DATE
Reflection is still open. Pick Reflection above to journal your thoughts.

Practice, Game, Instructor, and Gym entries are blocked for this date. To swap to one of those, scroll down to your entries list and delete the Off Day entry first.
😴 OFF DAY = FULL REST
No practice. No game. No instructor. No gym. Today is for rest, recovery, family, and the people in your corner. Off Day still counts toward your streak.

Want to journal your thoughts on top of the rest day? After you save this, switch the entry type to Reflection and log one too. Reflection is an addition, not a replacement.

Your stats are sourced from your Daily Journal.
Every game entry you log in the Daily Journal tab updates your season totals here — AVG, OBP, SLG, OPS, ERA, and WHIP all calculate on their own. The fields below are read-only — to add or edit a stat, log or edit a game entry on the Journal tab.
My Batting Stats
Is Your Average Real Yet?
Your Sample
— AB
Log a game in the Daily Journal to start the count.
Your average tells the truth only when you've had enough at-bats. A typical HS hitter gets 60–130 AB in a full season (20–30 games × 3–4 AB).
HS reliability tiers:
• Under 15 AB — way too small. One hit moves you 50 points.
• 15–39 AB — trend forming (about 5–13 games in).
• 40–79 AB — meaningful HS sample (~half a season).
• 80+ AB — solid HS workload. The number is real.
Example: .340 after 10 at-bats means almost nothing. .340 after 50 at-bats — now you're talking.
Sabermetric note: full statistical stability sits at ~910 AB (FanGraphs) — multi-year college / pro level, well beyond HS.
My Pitching Stats (pitchers only)
Is Your ERA Real Yet?
Your Sample
— IP
Pitch in a game and log it in the Daily Journal to start the count.
Pitching stats are noisier than batting. A typical HS pitcher logs 30–50 IP in a season (~1/3 of a 25-game schedule × 4–7 IP per start).
HS reliability tiers:
• Under 5 IP — way too small. One bad inning trashes the ERA.
• 5–14 IP — a few starts in, not stable yet.
• 15–29 IP — meaningful HS sample (~half-season of starts).
• 30+ IP — solid HS workload. The number is real.
Example: 1.50 ERA after 3 IP means almost nothing — that's 9 outs of luck. Same 1.50 over 25 IP? That's a real pitcher.
Sabermetric note: WHIP stabilizes around 100 IP, ERA around 300 IP (FanGraphs) — multi-year college / pro level, well beyond HS.
My Fielding Stats

Install MyGrind on Your Phone
One-tap access from your home screen. Loads instantly. Works offline. No App Store needed.
✓ Installed
MyGrind opens straight from your home screen.
Your browser will ask to confirm. Tap Install.
  1. Tap the Share button ⎋ at the bottom of Safari.
  2. In the menu that opens, swipe up and tap "Add to Home Screen".
  3. Tap Add. The MyGrind icon lands on your home screen.
Tip: The Share button is the square with an arrow pointing up. It lives at the bottom of Safari (or top-right on iPad).
Open MyGrind in Safari (iPhone/iPad) or Chrome (Android) to install. Other browsers don't support home-screen install.

Subscription & Billing
Free Trial Active
Active Subscription
Your subscription is active. You have full access to all features.
Parent Only · Uses billing email on file
Manage Subscription
Update card, change plan, or cancel anytime in the Stripe portal. Need help? Email support.
Cancellation Policy
Cancel anytime. No penalties, no fees. You keep access until the end of your current billing period.
Parent notification. The billing email on file (usually a parent) gets the cancellation confirmation by email. If you're a player and you cancel from inside the app, your parent will know. No surprise charges, no hidden moves.
90-day journal recovery. After cancellation we hold your journal (entries, stats, goals, grades) for 90 days. Re-subscribe within that window and everything comes back exactly where you left it. After 90 days, account data is permanently deleted.
Questions? Email support@mygrindapp.com

🎯
The 5 Tools
This game gives you a lifetime to chase something, and that is the whole joy of it. The five tools, hit, power, run, arm, and field, are simply how the game describes a complete player. Same five for baseball and softball, boys and girls. Pitchers get their own scale further down. Learn them, fall in love with the work, and chase your own ceiling. The numbers on these pages are a map for the dream, never a fence around it.
How to read these numbers
These are recruiting benchmarks, measured with today's tech (TrackMan, Rapsodo, Blast). They run a little hotter than the radar-gun numbers from 10 or 20 years ago, so read them as modern targets, not as a ceiling. They are ranges, not a single grade. Position changes everything: a catcher with a cannon gets a pass on speed, a center fielder does not. And every level starts at high school varsity here on purpose. Chasing radar-gun velo before then is how young arms get hurt (see Arm Health & Growth Plates in Resources).
1
Hit · For Average
The most important tool, and the hardest to fake. Hitting for average means squaring up good pitching and getting on base, at-bat after at-bat. This one is about production, not a lab number. A radar gun cannot tell you who comes through with two strikes and the game on the line.
What coaches actually watch
Quality at-bats. Works the count, controls the strike zone, does not chase.
Hard contact in games, not just in the cage.
A two-strike approach. Shortens up, battles, puts it in play.
Production against good pitching, not just weak arms.
In a box score it shows up as batting average and on-base percentage. Read those in context: a .320 in a tough league beats a .480 against weak pitching. Coaches watch the at-bat, not just the line.
💡 Coach read: Don't chase a batting average and don't chase a bat-speed number. Chase quality at-bats. Control the zone, hit it hard, and the production follows. Log every at-bat honest. The hitter who battles beats the hitter who just swings hard.
2
Power
What you do with contact in a game: driving balls into the gaps and over the fence, changing the score with one swing. The production is extra-base hits, home runs, and slugging, not a cage number. A big exit velocity says the power could be there. Doing it in games is what counts.
What coaches actually watch
Extra-base hits and home runs in real games.
Slugging and driving in runs, not empty solo shots.
Power to all fields, not only pull-side.
Damage against good pitching.
The raw tool underneath is exit velocity, measured at showcases off a tee. For reference, college bats often show tee numbers in the 90s and up.tee numbers around 65 to 75 mph and up. Treat it as the floor of your potential, not your production.
💡 Coach read: Exit velo gets you measured. Slugging gets you remembered. Build the strength for raw power, then take it into the game. Production over the radar gun, every time.
3
Run · Speed
Speed shows up on defense, on the bases, and in your range. Baseball clocks it with the 60-yard dash. Softball uses home-to-first time off a swing, because the bases are closer and the first step matters more. Lower is faster in both.
Level ⚾ 60-Yd Dash 🥎 Home-to-1B
HS Varsity7.0–7.5 s
under 6.9 = above avg		2.8–2.9 s
Junior College~7.0 s3.0–3.2 s
4-Yr College6.5–6.8 s
D1		2.7–3.0 s
D1 elite 2.7–2.8
Log your 60 on your Profile and watch it trend over time.Slappers run faster off a left-side running start: under 2.9 is competitive, under 2.7 is elite. Log your home-to-first on your Profile and watch it trend.
💡 Coach read: Speed is the one tool you can improve with no risk to your arm. Sprint mechanics, first-step quickness, and lower-body strength all stack. Track it.
4
Arm · Throwing
Arm strength is throwing velocity for position players, and for catchers it is also pop time, the seconds from the catch to the ball reaching second base. Softball arm velos run lower than baseball, that is normal and expected. Build the arm with daily care and long toss, never by maxing out.
Throwing velocity (OF / IF)
Level ⚾ Baseball 🥎 Softball
HS Varsity70–80 mph50–60 mph
Junior College~78+ mph52–61 mph
4-Yr College85–95+ mph
D1 OF 87+		55–64 mph
D1
Catcher pop time to 2B (lower = better)
Level ⚾ Baseball 🥎 Softball
HS Varsity2.1–2.25 s2.1–2.2 s
Junior College~2.0–2.1 s~1.85–2.1 s
4-Yr College1.95 s or faster
D1		1.8–2.0 s
D1
Softball's second base sits closer than baseball's, so the pop times look similar even though the throw is shorter.
💡 Coach read: A strong arm is built, not born, and it is also the easiest tool to ruin. J-Bands every day, long toss with a plan, and rest. Read the Growth Plates card before you ever chase a velo number.
5
Field · Defense
This is the tool with no clean number. Fielding percentage barely moves at the top because everyone makes the routine play. What coaches actually grade is what a stat line never shows: first-step reads, footwork, hands, range, and the transfer from glove to throwing hand. The eye test wins here.
What scouts look for at every level: gets to balls others can't (range), clean exchange and quick release, soft and quiet hands, and reliability on the routine play under pressure. The same standard applies in baseball and softball. Track your putouts, assists, and errors in your Journal so the trend is honest, but know the glove is judged with the eyes.
💡 Coach read: Defense is the tool that keeps you on the field while your bat catches up. It is all reps and footwork. No radar gun needed, and no excuse not to work it.
⚾
Pitching · Its Own Tool
Pitchers are graded on their own scale, not the five position tools. Velocity gets the first look, but command and a second pitch keep you on the mound. Baseball measures the overhand fastball. Softball measures the windmill, which tops out lower because the motion is different, not weaker.
Level ⚾ Fastball 🥎 Windmill
HS Varsity75–83 mph52–58 mph
Junior College~80–88 mph55–62 mph
4-Yr College87–95 mph
D1		57–64 mph
D1 · Power-5 starters 66–70
💡 Coach read: Velo opens the door. Command and a real second pitch keep you in the room. And no pitcher ever regretted respecting their pitch count. Protect the arm first, always.
Which tools matter for your spot
No one is elite at all five. Coaches recruit the tools your position needs most.

Catcher: arm and pop time first, then hit. Speed gets a pass.
Middle infield (SS / 2B): field and arm, with speed close behind.
Center field: run and arm. You cover the most ground on the field.
Corner outfield / 1B / 3B: hit and power carry the load.
Pitcher: its own scale. Velo, command, second pitch.

Find the two tools your position lives on, then make them undeniable.
💡 Pro tip from Coach: These numbers are a map, not a verdict. Late bloomers pass early risers every single year. Log the work, chase the next number, and let the body grow on its own clock.
Resources
Bookmark this page. The links college coaches, recruiters, and parents ask about every season, plus the fastest way to get help inside the app.
Eligibility Centers
Start Sophomore Year
Paperwork that lets you play college ball. Open these the year before you think you need to.
NCAA Eligibility Center →
Required · D1/D2
Non-negotiable for D1 + D2. Most players who get burned waited until junior year. Don't.
NAIA Eligibility Center →
Required · NAIA
Same idea as NCAA but for NAIA programs. Different system, just as required.
NJCAA (Junior College) →
2-Year Path
JuCo is a real path. Plenty of D1 starters spent two years here first.
NCAA Recruiting Calendar →
Reference
When coaches can call, text, or watch you play. Re-read it every August.
💡 Pro tip from Coach: NCAA and NAIA accounts can be opened the same day. Knock both out sophomore fall and you're done thinking about it.
Showcases & Rankings
Where Coaches Look
Get on the right list early. A profile beats a highlight reel a coach has to hunt for.
⚾ Perfect Game →
Baseball · National
The biggest national platform. Profile, showcases, rankings, and recruit data all live here.
⚾ Prep Baseball Report (PBR) →
Baseball · State
State-by-state coverage. Strong for in-state coaches and regional showcases.
🥎 Extra Inning Softball →
Softball · National
National softball rankings, events, and recruit-board coverage. Bookmark for tournament season.
🥎 NFCA (Softball Coaches) →
Softball · Directory
Find any college softball coach by school. Searchable, current contacts.
🥎 Premier Girls Fastpitch (PGF) →
Softball · Showcases
The premier national showcase circuit. Camps and events put you in front of the top college coaches.
🥎 The Alliance Fastpitch →
Softball · Rankings
National league with team rankings across nine regions. Tournaments from 10U to 18U.
💡 Pro tip from Coach: Build your profile before your first showcase. Coaches scan profiles in the bleachers. No profile, no callback.
Girls in Baseball
Big Year · Growing Fast
Girls baseball is having a big year. The Women's Pro Baseball League launches August 2026 as the first U.S. pro league. College club teams are growing at Cal, UC Davis, USC, Washington, Occidental, and more, with NCAA Emerging Sport status next. The game is opening up.
⚾ Women's Pro Baseball League →
Pro · Aug 2026
First U.S. women's pro baseball league ever. Teams in Boston, NY, LA, and SF. Mo'ne Davis on the LA roster. Inaugural season starts August 1, 2026.
⚾ Baseball For All →
Community · College Clubs
The leading nonprofit. Tournaments, summer camps, and the growing Women's College Club Baseball circuit (Cal, UC Davis, Washington, and more).
⚾ USA Baseball Women's Team →
Elite · International
National Team trials, development program, and the Women's Baseball World Cup pipeline.
⚾ MLB Develops →
Programs · Showcases
MLB-sponsored development programs. The Trailblazer Series showcase for girls 11-13 lives here.
⚾ Women in Baseball (Hall of Fame) →
History · Inspiration
The AAGPBL and beyond. Know the women who came before. Tap "Discover" then search Women in Baseball.
⚾ LA Heat Girls' Baseball →
SoCal · Free Clinics
LA-based nonprofit. Free skills clinics run by women coaches plus all-girl tournament teams. Founded by players, for players.
💡 Pro tip from Coach: Watch WPBL games this August to see what's possible. Then go through Baseball For All for community, tournaments, and the college club circuit. LA Heat for SoCal clinics.
Academic & Financial Aid
Grades + Tests Move the Needle
Bat speed gets you a look. GPA and SAT get you the scholarship.
📘 Khan Academy SAT Prep →
Free · Official
Free, College Board partnered SAT practice. Hundreds of hours of content, score-personalized.
📝 Common App →
1,000+ Colleges
One application, one essay, sent to up to 20 colleges. Saves weeks every senior year.
💰 FAFSA →
Federal Aid
Federal student aid form. Opens Oct 1 every year. Fill it out fast. Money is first-come, first-served.
🎓 College Board →
SAT · College Search
SAT registration, score sends, and college search by program, region, or major.
💡 Pro tip from Coach: A 3.5 GPA plus 1100 SAT opens doors a 4.0 GPA alone won't. Coaches see both numbers.
🦴 Arm Health & Growth Plates
Read This Young
The most common serious injuries in youth baseball and softball are also the most preventable. Almost all of them trace back to the growth plate. Know what it is before you chase a number.
What a growth plate is
A growth plate is a soft layer of cartilage near the ends of a young athlete's bones, where the bone is still growing. It stays open until the late teens. Here is the part that matters: while it is open, it is the weakest link in the joint, weaker than the ligaments and tendons around it. A force that would only strain a grown adult can crack or slip a kid's growth plate instead.
The injuries you actually see
Little League elbow and Little League shoulder are growth-plate overuse injuries from too much throwing. Osgood-Schlatter (knee) and Sever's (heel) come from running and jumping. Softball windmill pitching is easier on the elbow than overhand, but high pitch counts still load the shoulder, back, and hip the same way. Different sport, same rule.
What causes it
Overuse. Throwing year round with no off-season. Chasing radar-gun velocity before the body is built for it. Pitching through arm pain. None of these show up as one big moment. They build up quietly, then the growth plate gives.
How you protect it
Respect pitch counts and rest days, scaled to your age. Take two to four months off from throwing every year. Do your daily arm care (the checklist lives in your Journal). And treat arm pain as information, not weakness. Sharp or lasting pain in a young elbow or shoulder is never something to push through.
⚾ USA Baseball Pitch Smart →
Pitch Counts · By Age
The official pitch-count and rest-day guidelines by age. The single best free resource for keeping a young arm healthy.
🥎 Windmill Pitch Counts
Sports-Med Guidance
Softball has no single official standard like baseball's, but sports-medicine groups recommend roughly 50 to 100 pitches per game depending on age, with rest days between starts. The windmill is easier on the elbow than overhand, but it still loads the shoulder, back, and hip. Manage the innings, watch for pain, and rest the arm.
This is education, not medical advice. If a young player has arm, elbow, shoulder, knee, or heel pain that is sharp, swelling, or lasts more than a few days, stop and see a doctor or athletic trainer. Growth-plate injuries caught early almost always heal. Ignored, some do not.
💡 Pro tip from Coach: This is why our benchmark numbers start at high school varsity. Before that, the goal is not velo. It is staying healthy long enough to be great when it counts.
